card.intuition: Every token looks at every other token and decides how much to 'attend' to each one — producing a context-aware representation that captures long-range dependencies in a single layer.
card.analogy: In a meeting, before you answer a question, you briefly glance at everyone in the room and decide whose earlier comment is most relevant to your reply — and you do this simultaneously for every participant.
card.time_complexity: O(n² × d) — quadratic in sequence length n, linear in dimension d
card.space_complexity: O(n² + n × d) for attention scores and output
when_to_use: Processing sequences where long-range context matters (language, code, DNA)
When you need parallelizable training (unlike RNNs, attention has no sequential dependency)
As the core building block of any Transformer-based model (BERT, GPT, T5, etc.)
gotchas: Quadratic cost in sequence length: attention on a 10k-token sequence is 100× more expensive than on a 1k-token one — this is why context windows were limited for years
Attention alone has no notion of token order — positional encodings must be added explicitly
In decoder (causal) attention, future tokens must be masked — forgetting this leaks future information during training

exercise.prompt: Complete the scaled dot-product attention function — the core operation inside every Transformer layer.
exercise.starter_code:
import numpy as np

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q, K, V: (seq_len, d_k) matrices
    Returns: (seq_len, d_k) attended output
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]

    # Step 1: compute raw attention scores
    scores = ___________          # (seq_len, seq_len)

    # Step 2: scale to prevent softmax saturation
    scores = scores / ___________

    # Step 3: apply mask (set masked positions to -inf)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))

    # Step 4: softmax over key dimension
    weights = np.exp(scores) / np.exp(scores).sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True)

    # Step 5: weighted sum of values
    return ___________
exercise.solution:
scores = Q @ K.T          # dot product of queries with all keys
scores / np.sqrt(d_k)     # scale by sqrt(d_k)
weights @ V               # weighted sum of value vectors
hints: Attention scores = Q · Kᵀ. Each query 'asks a question' and each key 'offers an answer' — the dot product measures compatibility
Scale by √d_k because large d_k makes dot products grow large, pushing softmax into near-zero gradient regions
The output is a weighted sum of Value vectors — high-attention tokens contribute more to the final representation
exercise.test_code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)
seq,dk = 4,8
Q=np.random.randn(seq,dk); K=np.random.randn(seq,dk); V=np.random.randn(seq,dk)
out = scaled_dot_product_attention(Q,K,V)
assert out.shape==(seq,dk), f'Expected ({seq},{dk}), got {out.shape}'
assert not np.any(np.isnan(out)), 'Output has NaN'
assert not np.any(np.isinf(out)), 'Output has Inf'
print('PASS')
